title: Brigadeiro
subtitle: Brazilian chocolate condensed milk truffles
excerpt: Three ingredients cooked in a saucepan: condensed milk, cocoa powder, and butter. Stirred over medium heat until the mixture pulls away from the pan, then rolled into balls once cool and coated in cocoa powder. A standard at Brazilian birthday celebrations. No oven required.
sourceNotes: Brigadeiro is a Brazilian confection created in the mid-twentieth century, traditionally attributed to the election campaign of Brigadeiro Eduardo Gomes in the 1940s. It became one of the most widely made and eaten sweets in Brazil and is now found throughout the Brazilian diaspora. The recipe uses three core ingredients cooked on the hob to produce a soft fudge-like truffle, rolled in chocolate sprinkles or cocoa powder.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch in a wide pan; the mixture will take longer to thicken.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Brigadeiro keeps at room temperature for up to 3 days in a covered container. Do not refrigerate; condensation softens the coating.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven; cooked on the hob over medium heat

glossaryTerms.definition: The brigadeiro mixture is ready when it pulls away cleanly from the base and sides of the pan as you stir, leaving the pan surface exposed for a second before the mixture flows back. This takes 10 to 15 minutes over medium heat. Under-cooked brigadeiro is too soft to roll once cool; over-cooked is dry and crumbly. The pull-away test is more reliable than timing because pan size and heat level vary.

content.text: Brigadeiro requires constant stirring and patience. The
tells you when to stop cooking. Do not rush the heat: too high and the mixture scorches on the bottom before it thickens properly.

Put 397 g condensed milk, 3 tbsp cocoa powder, and 15 g butter in a heavy-based saucepan. Stir to combine over medium heat. Cook, stirring constantly with a silicone spatula, for 10 to 15 minutes until the mixture thickens and begins to pull away from the base and sides of the pan. Pour onto a lightly buttered plate and spread into a shallow layer. Leave to cool to room temperature, about 1 hour. Do not refrigerate; the mixture needs to be cool but pliable.

Spread 4 tbsp cocoa powder on a plate. Rub a little butter on your hands. Scoop heaped teaspoons of the cooled mixture and roll into smooth balls. Roll each ball in the cocoa powder to coat. Place in small paper cases if serving at a party. Store at room temperature.

items.problem: Mixture is too soft to roll after cooling
items.solution: The mixture was not cooked long enough. Return it to the pan and cook for a further 5 minutes, stirring constantly, until it pulls away from the pan more decisively. Spread out again and cool before rolling.

items.problem: Mixture scorched on the bottom of the pan
items.solution: The heat was too high. Use medium heat throughout and do not stop stirring. A heavy-based pan distributes heat more evenly than a thin one.
